taskDescription: Drag the correct anatomical terms into the blanks to complete statements about the temporal and infratemporal regions.
textField:
All four muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, and lateral pterygoid) are supplied by the *mandibular* division (V3) of the trigeminal nerve. The masseter and *temporalis* are powerful elevators of the mandible. The medial pterygoid assists in *elevation* and side-to-side grinding movements.

The only muscle of mastication that can depress (open) the mandible is the *lateral* pterygoid, which has two heads: a superior head from the greater wing of the sphenoid and an inferior head from the lateral pterygoid *plate*. It also protrudes the mandible and assists in lateral excursion.

The maxillary artery is the terminal branch of the *external* carotid artery and is the dominant vessel of the infratemporal fossa. It has three parts in relation to the lateral pterygoid and gives off the *middle* meningeal artery (which enters the skull through the foramen *spinosum*) and the inferior *alveolar* artery.

The pterygoid venous plexus in the infratemporal fossa communicates with the *cavernous* sinus via emissary veins, providing a dangerous route for spread of dental *infections* intracranially. The plexus also communicates with the *facial* vein.

In TMJ dislocation, the mandibular condyle slips anterior to the *articular* eminence and is locked by spasm of the lateral pterygoid. The TMJ disc divides the joint into upper and lower *compartments*.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the temporal and infratemporal region anatomy.
Good effort! Revise the muscles and vessels.
Excellent understanding of the infratemporal fossa!
